title: Focaccia with rosemary and sea salt
subtitle: Flat Italian bread soaked in olive oil with a dimpled crust
excerpt: Focaccia is a high-hydration dough that ferments slowly, then bakes in a well-oiled tin. The dimpling before it goes in the oven is not decorative, the indentations collect olive oil and produce the characteristic crunchy-edged, chewy-centred crust.
sourceNotes: Focaccia is a Ligurian flatbread documented in Italian culinary literature from at least the medieval period. The rosemary-and-sea-salt version is the classic Genoese form, olive oil, coarse salt, and fresh rosemary are the definitive toppings. Widely documented in twentieth-century Italian domestic cookery translated for British audiences.

content.text: Focaccia is more forgiving than most breads, the high hydration makes it sticky to handle, but it barely needs shaping. The structure comes from a series of stretch-and-folds during bulk fermentation rather than kneading.
the dough just before baking is the step that defines the finished texture: it creates the uneven surface, holds the olive oil, and anchors the rosemary.

Combine {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, and {{salt-table}}. Add {{water}} and 2 tbsp of the {{olive-oil}}. Mix until no dry flour remains, the dough will be very wet and sticky. Cover and leave for 30 minutes. Perform a set of stretch-and-folds: wet your hand, reach under the dough, stretch it up and fold it over. Rotate the bowl a quarter turn and repeat three more times. Cover and rest 30 minutes. Repeat this set twice more at 30-minute intervals. After the third set the dough should feel noticeably stronger and hold its shape better.

Pour the remaining {{olive-oil}} into a large baking tin (roughly 30 × 40 cm) and tilt to coat. Tip the dough in and turn it gently to coat in the oil. Leave it as a rough shape, don't press it to the edges yet. Cover and rest for 30 minutes, then use your fingers to coax the dough to the corners of the tin. It will resist at first; leave it to relax for another 15 minutes and finish spreading it then. Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan).

Press all ten fingers firmly into the dough to dimple it all over, right down to the base of the tin. Drizzle with a little extra olive oil, scatter the {{rosemary}} leaves, and finish with {{sea-salt-flakes}}. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until deep gold. The underside should be crisp when you lift a corner with a palette knife. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes, then slide onto a wire rack.
